title: Ezekiel 28
subtitle: The King of Tyre and the Promise of Restoration

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Siege of Tyre

sections.header: Verses 1-10 — The Prince of Tyre: Human Pride

heb.paragraph: The prince of Tyre declares 'I am a god' (el ani, v.2). God’s response is devastating: 'You are a man, not a god' — the fundamental distinction that human pride seeks to erase.

refs.note: The serpent’s original temptation: 'you will be like God.' The prince of Tyre repeats the primal sin.
The king of Babylon declares 'I will make myself like the Most High.'
Herod Agrippa accepts divine acclamation and is struck down.

notes.note: The prince claims to be 'a god, sitting on the throne of a god.' Ultimate hubris.
The reference to Daniel may refer to the legendary wise man of Ugaritic texts or the biblical Daniel.
The 'most ruthless of nations' will strip the prince of his beauty and cast him into the pit.
The devastating question: 'Will you then say I am a god in the presence of those who kill you?'

Calvin sees the prince as the archetype of human pride: wealth leads to self-sufficiency, then self-deification. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

Calvin: Death is the great equaliser that strips all pretensions of divinity. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Hebrew nagid ('prince') rather than melek ('king') may be a deliberate demotion. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block identifies this as a 'hubris oracle' with deep ancient Near Eastern parallels. Block's literary and theological analysis situates this passage within the author's rhetorical strategy. The text functions as both narrative and argument — the story advances the book's theological thesis about the consequences of covenant faithfulness and unfaithfulness in Israel's life under God.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli reads this as a 'wisdom-critical' text: the prince’s wisdom is real but misused. Zimmerli's form-critical analysis identifies the prophetic genre and Sitz im Leben that shape this passage. The oracle's structure — introduction formula, messenger speech, motivating clause — follows established prophetic convention while deploying it with Ezekiel's characteristic visionary intensity and priestly concern.

hist.context: The oracle shifts from Tyre as a city to Tyre’s ruler personally. The 'prince' (nagid) claims divine status because of his wisdom in amassing wealth. God establishes the fundamental Creator-creature distinction.

sections.header: Verses 11-19 — The King of Tyre: Eden and the Fall

heb.paragraph: The king was 'an anointed guardian cherub' on 'the holy mountain of God.' The cherub language connects to the Eden narrative (Gen 3:24) and the tabernacle theology (Exod 25:18-22).
The king is placed 'in Eden, the garden of God.' The Eden imagery elevates the oracle beyond historical commentary into theological territory.

refs.note: The Eden imagery directly connects to Genesis 3: original perfection, guardian cherub, fall through pride, and expulsion.
The 'morning star' passage parallels Ezekiel 28: original glory, fall through pride. Both are traditionally read as reflecting Satan’s fall.
Jesus says 'I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven.'
The war in heaven and Satan’s expulsion echoes the expulsion from the mountain of God.

notes.note: The lament uses superlative language: 'You were the seal of perfection, full of wisdom and perfect in beauty.'
The nine precious stones echo the high priest’s breastplate (Exod 28:17-20) in different order.
The 'anointed cherub who covers' uses tabernacle language. Many see a reference to Satan’s original exalted position.
Blameless until wickedness was found. The fall pattern: beauty to vanity, wisdom to pride, trade to violence.
Calvin is cautious about the Satan interpretation, reading this primarily as hyperbolic language while acknowledging the language transcends any merely human referent.
Calvin: Beauty, when it breeds self-admiration, becomes the seed of its own destruction.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The MT lists nine stones; the LXX lists twelve, matching the high priest’s breastplate. The LXX may preserve an earlier reading. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

The phrase 'anointed guardian cherub' is extremely difficult. The word mimshakh may mean 'anointed' or 'outspread.'. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli argues this draws on a pre-Israelite myth of a primordial man in paradise — not identical to Genesis 2-3 but from the same tradition pool. Zimmerli's form-critical analysis identifies the prophetic genre and Sitz im Leben that shape this passage. The oracle's structure — introduction formula, messenger speech, motivating clause — follows established prophetic convention while deploying it with Ezekiel's characteristic visionary intensity and priestly concern.

hist.context: This is one of the most debated passages in Ezekiel. The 'king' (melek, now elevated from 'prince') is described in Edenic and angelic language. Many interpreters have read this as a description of Satan’s fall; others read it as mythological language applied to a human ruler.

sections.header: Verses 20-26 — Oracle Against Sidon and Israel’s Restoration

heb.paragraph: God declares 'I will be proved holy' (niqdashti) through Sidon’s judgment. The niphal form indicates that God’s holiness is demonstrated through his acts of justice.

refs.note: The restoration promise anticipates the fuller vision of Ezekiel 36.
Jeremiah’s parallel promise: Jacob will return, live in peace.
Zechariah’s vision of Jerusalem without walls — God himself will be a wall of fire.

notes.note: God will 'gain glory' through Sidon’s judgment. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

Calvin reads the restoration as ultimately fulfilled in Christ and the church. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase 'gathered from the nations' uses ingathering language that becomes technical for eschatological restoration. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

hist.context: The brief Sidon oracle functions as a transition from the Tyre oracles to the restoration promise. The chapter concludes with the first explicit restoration oracle since the judgment sections began.

themes.note: Sovereignty dominates as God brings down the proudest human claims to divinity. The mercy score rises with the restoration promise.

rows.label: vv.11-19
rows.text: The King of Tyre: Edenic Fall

lit.note: The chapter moves from human pride to cosmic fall to the first restoration promise, pivoting the book from judgment toward hope.

hebtext.note: קָדַשְׁתִּי (qadashti) = 'I have been holy' or 'I am holy' (qal perfect 1st person)
The king was 'an anointed guardian cherub' on 'the holy mountain of God.' The cherub language connects to the Eden narrative (Gen 3:24) and the tabernacle theology (Exod 25:18-22).
The king is placed 'in Eden, the garden of God.' The Eden imagery elevates the oracle beyond historical commentary into theological territory.
God declares 'I will be proved holy' (niqdashti) through Sidon’s judgment. The niphal form indicates that God’s holiness is demonstrated through his acts of justice.

trans.title: Comparing translations in The King of Tyre and the Promise of Restoration

translations.text: “ ‘Therefore this is what the Sovereign Lord says: “ ‘Because you think you are wise, as wise as a god,
therefore thus says the Lord GOD: Because you make your heart like the heart of a god,
I am going to bring foreigners against you, the most ruthless of nations; they will draw their swords against your beauty and wisdom and pierce your shining splendor.
therefore, behold, I will bring foreigners upon you, the most ruthless of the nations; and they shall draw their swords against the beauty of your wisdom and defile your splendor.

thread.text: Jesus says 'I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven.'

coaching.tip: The prince of Tyre claims 'I am a god; I sit on the throne of a god' (v. 2). God's response: 'You are a mere mortal, not a god.' The sin isn't wealth or success — it's the category confusion between creator and creature. Human beings who forget they're human invite correction. The oracle isn't about Tyre specifically; it's about what happens when any creature claims the creator's seat.

The 'king of Tyre' passage (vv. 12–19) describes a figure 'in Eden, the garden of God,' adorned with every precious stone, blameless until 'wickedness was found in you.' Like Isaiah 14:12–15, this transcends the historical referent. The passage became the basis for later traditions about Satan's fall — an angelic being who traded heavenly beauty for pride. Historical oracle and cosmic drama occupy the same verses.

prayer_prompt: As the king of Tyre says 'I am a god; I sit on the throne of a god' and is brought down, consider asking God whether any success in your life has produced the kind of pride that forgets it was given.
